concept.headline: Elixir strings are double-quoted UTF-8 binaries. Interpolation uses #{expr}. The String module provides length, upcase, downcase, split, trim, contains?, and more.
concept.explanation: Elixir strings are UTF-8 encoded binaries (<< >>). Always use double quotes — single-quoted values are charlists (lists of code points), a different type. String interpolation embeds any Elixir expression with #{expr}. The String module functions always return new strings (immutable). String.length/1 counts Unicode grapheme clusters; byte_size/1 counts raw bytes.
concept.syntax:
# String literals — DOUBLE quotes only
name = "Alice"

# Interpolation
"Hello, #{name}!"          # Hello, Alice!
"1 + 1 = #{1 + 1}"        # 1 + 1 = 2

# String module
String.length("hello")    # 5
String.upcase("hello")    # HELLO
String.downcase("HELLO")  # hello
String.trim("  hi  ")     # hi
String.split("a,b,c", ",") # ["a", "b", "c"]
String.contains?("hello", "ell")  # true

# Concatenation
"Hello" <> " " <> "World"  # Hello World
keyPoints: Double-quoted ONLY — single quotes create charlists, not strings
Interpolation: #{expr} — any expression inside the braces
String.length counts characters; byte_size counts bytes

demo.intro: Watch string interpolation, concatenation, and String module functions in action.
demo.code:
name = "Alice"
score = 95
greeting = "Hello, #{name}!"
IO.puts(greeting)
msg = "Score: #{score * 2}"
IO.puts(msg)
IO.puts(String.length(name))
IO.puts(String.upcase(name))
parts = String.split("a,b,c", ",")
IO.inspect(parts)

explanation.what: name = "Alice" — double-quoted string bound to name
explanation.why: Double quotes create a UTF-8 binary string in Elixir
explanation.misconception: NEVER use single quotes for strings in Elixir. 'Alice' creates a charlist [65, 108, 105, 99, 101] — a list of Unicode code point integers. This is a completely different type from the binary string "Alice".

explanation.what: score = 95 — integer for use in interpolation
explanation.why: Setting up interpolation demo with a non-string value
explanation.intent: Show that interpolation works with any type
explanation.misconception: #{expr} in an interpolated string converts any expression result to its string representation automatically — no manual Integer.to_string() needed.

explanation.what: "Hello, #{name}!" — #{name} is replaced with the value of name
explanation.why: String interpolation evaluates #{expr} and embeds the result
explanation.intent: Show basic interpolation with a variable
explanation.misconception: #{} evaluates ANY Elixir expression — not just variables. #{name} evaluates the variable name. #{1 + 1} evaluates the expression 1+1 → 2.

explanation.why: The interpolated string was constructed with name='Alice'
explanation.misconception: The interpolated string is a NEW string — it is not dynamically updated if name changes later. Elixir strings are constructed once at the point of evaluation.

explanation.why: Interpolation can embed arbitrary expressions, not just variables
explanation.misconception: score * 2 is fully evaluated (95 * 2 = 190) THEN converted to string and embedded. The interpolation syntax #{} is an expression boundary — any valid Elixir expression works.
score itself is unchanged — it is still 95. The expression score * 2 was evaluated and embedded in the new string, not stored back.
String.length counts GRAPHEME CLUSTERS (visible characters), not bytes. For ASCII strings these are the same. But "café" has String.length 4 but byte_size 5 (é is 2 bytes in UTF-8).

explanation.why: String.upcase creates a new uppercased string
explanation.intent: Show String.upcase returning a new string
explanation.misconception: String.upcase returns a NEW string — name is still 'Alice'. All String module functions return new strings (immutable values).

explanation.why: Splits on the delimiter "," and returns a list of substrings
explanation.misconception: String.split returns a List, not a string. You can iterate it with Enum.each or destructure it with pattern matching.

explanation.why: IO.inspect shows the Elixir representation including quotes
explanation.intent: Show IO.inspect vs IO.puts for lists
explanation.misconception: IO.puts on a list of strings would concatenate them without separators. IO.inspect is better for debugging non-scalar values — it shows the Elixir representation faithfully.

prediction.question:
What is the output?

lang = "Elixir"
IO.puts("I love #{String.downcase(lang)}!")
prediction.explanation: String.downcase("Elixir") → "elixir". The interpolation #{...} embeds the result. Output: I love elixir!

explanation.afterDemo: String interpolation with #{} is idiomatic Elixir — preferred over string concatenation with <>. It handles type conversion automatically and is more readable. The String module covers most common operations; for high-performance binary manipulation, Elixir also has the binary pattern matching syntax (<< >>) which is powerful for parsing protocols and files.
keyTakeaways: Double-quoted strings only — single quotes are charlists.
#{expr} interpolates any expression — variable, arithmetic, function call.
<> concatenates strings. String module for transformations (all return new strings).

practice.prompt: Bind name = "elixir" and year = 2011. Print "Elixir was created in 2011" using interpolation and String.capitalize/1 for the name.
practice.starterCode:
name = "elixir"
year = 2011
IO.puts("#{String.capitalize(name)} was created in #{year}")
practice.solutionCode:
name = "elixir"
year = 2011
IO.puts("#{String.capitalize(name)} was created in #{year}")
practice.hint: String.capitalize(name) → "Elixir". Embed both in a single interpolated string.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! String.capitalize makes 'elixir' → 'Elixir', interpolated with year 2011.

summary.title: Strings — Complete
takeaways: Double-quoted UTF-8 strings. Single quotes = charlists (different type).
#{expr} interpolation — any expression, automatic type conversion.
String module: length, upcase, downcase, trim, split, contains? — all return new strings.
